aiTitle: Debug vsim DMI vector register support

content.text: Now the PB infrastructure that already exists. Let me read the encoders/PB API and find where staging is wired in.

content.content:
1	// Program Buffer execution helpers for the RISC-V Debug Module.
2	//
3	// Follows the same duck-typed template pattern as dmi_poll.hpp so that
4	// resume_driver.hpp can include this header without a circular dependency.
5	// Bus type requirement: write(uint8_t reg, uint32_t val) and
6	//                       read(uint8_t reg) -> uint32_t.
7	//
8	// Debug Spec references: section 3.6 (Program Buffer), table 3.1 (DMI),
9	// section 3.7.1.3 (Abstract Command - Access Register).
10	
11	#pragma once
12	
13	#include "hybrid/abstract_command.hpp"
14	#include "hybrid/dmi_poll.hpp"
15	
16	#include <cstdint>
17	#include <span>
18	#include <stdexcept>
19	#include <string>
20	
21	namespace hybrid {
22	
23	// DMI address of the first Program Buffer register (progbuf0..progbuf15).
24	inline constexpr uint8_t kProgbuf0 = 0x20u;
25	
26	// Max payload instructions: 16 total slots minus 1 reserved for the
27	// mandatory ebreak that terminates execution.
28	inline constexpr std::size_t kProgbufMaxInsns = 15u;
29	
30	// Standard ebreak encoding (funct12=1, opcode=SYSTEM).
31	inline constexpr uint32_t kEbreak = 0x00100073u;
32	
33	// "Execute Program Buffer only" command (Debug Spec table 3.1):
34	//   cmdtype  = 0 (Access Register)
35	//   aarsize  = Bits64 (3) - ignored by DM when transfer=0, set for consistency
36	//   postexec = 1 (bit 18)
37	//   transfer = 0 (bit 17 NOT set) - skip register I/O, only run progbuf
38	//   write    = 0 (irrelevant when transfer=0)
39	//   regno    = 0 (irrelevant when transfer=0)
40	inline constexpr uint32_t kExecProgbufCmd =
41	    (uint32_t(AarSize::Bits64) & 0x7u) << 20 | (1u << 18);
42	
43	// Encode `vsetvl rd, rs1, rs2` (RVV 1.0 section 6.2, funct7=1000000).
44	// Tier-B restore: stage vl in rs1 and vtype in rs2 via Abstract Commands,
45	// then branch-free-restore both in one Program Buffer execution.
46	inline constexpr uint32_t encode_vsetvl(uint8_t rd, uint8_t rs1, uint8_t rs2)
47	{
48	    return (0b1000000u << 25)
49	         | ((uint32_t(rs2) & 0x1Fu) << 20)
50	         | ((uint32_t(rs1) & 0x1Fu) << 15)
51	         | (0b111u << 12)
52	         | ((uint32_t(rd)  & 0x1Fu) << 7)
53	         | 0x57u;  // OP-V opcode
54	}
55	
56	// Encode `csrrw rd, csr, rs1` (SYSTEM opcode, funct3=001).
57	// Tier-A PB fallback: stage the CSR value in rs1 via Abstract Command,
58	// then write it to a CSR that the DM cannot reach via abstract access.
59	inline constexpr uint32_t encode_csrrw(uint8_t rd, uint16_t csr, uint8_t rs1)
60	{
61	    return ((uint32_t(csr) & 0xFFFu) << 20)
62	         | ((uint32_t(rs1) & 0x1Fu) << 15)
63	         | (0b001u << 12)
64	         | ((uint32_t(rd)  & 0x1Fu) << 7)
65	         | 0x73u;  // SYSTEM opcode
66	}
67	
68	// Write insns[0..n-1] to progbuf0..n-1 and kEbreak to progbuf[n].
69	// Clears any sticky abstractcs.cmderr (W1C), issues kExecProgbufCmd, polls
70	// until abstractcs.busy clears, then returns whether cmderr stayed clear.
71	//
72	// Returns true on success; false if the DM set cmderr during execution.
73	// Throws std::runtime_error if insns.size() > kProgbufMaxInsns or poll
74	// times out.
75	template<typename Bus>
76	inline bool exec_progbuf(Bus& bus, std::span<const uint32_t> insns)
77	{
78	    constexpr uint8_t  kAbstractcs   = 0x16u;
79	    constexpr uint8_t  kCommand      = 0x17u;
80	    constexpr uint32_t kBusy         = 1u << 12;
81	    constexpr uint32_t kCmderrMask   = 0x7u << 8;
82	
83	    if (insns.size() > kProgbufMaxInsns)
84	        throw std::runtime_error(
85	            "exec_progbuf: too many instructions ("
86	            + std::to_string(insns.size()) + " > "
87	            + std::to_string(kProgbufMaxInsns) + ")");
88	
89	    for (std::size_t i = 0; i < insns.size(); ++i)
90	        bus.write(static_cast<uint8_t>(kProgbuf0 + i), insns[i]);
91	    bus.write(static_cast<uint8_t>(kProgbuf0 + insns.size()), kEbreak);
92	
93	    if ((bus.read(kAbstractcs) & kCmderrMask) != 0u)
94	        bus.write(kAbstractcs, kCmderrMask);
95	
96	    bus.write(kCommand, kExecProgbufCmd);
97	    poll_until_clear(bus, kAbstractcs, kBusy, "exec_progbuf busy");
98	
99	    return (bus.read(kAbstractcs) & kCmderrMask) == 0u;
100	}
101	
102	}  // namespace hybrid
103

2. Does ANY existing C++ code in verilator/src or verilator/tests already encode the RISC-V vector instructions `vle8.v`, `vse8.v`, or `vsetvli` (I-form, immediate vtype)? 3. How does the vsim Debug Module / CPU wrapper expose the Program Buffer size (progbufsize) and abstract-command capabilities? 4. Does anything set or reference dcsr.stopcount / dcsr (CSR 0x7B0) - i.e. does executing instructions via the Program Buffer in debug mode advance mcycle/minstret on this DM, or are counters frozen while halted? Grep for dcsr, 0x7b0, stopcount, "debug mode", count.

5. Find the DPI "set_ilm" pattern referenced by the plan: search verilator/src/cpu_wrappers (or similar) for set_ilm and any existing DPI backdoor that mirrors guest memory or regfile state. Report the file:line and a 2-3 line summary of what mechanism it uses (so I can compare PB vs DPI).

aiTitle: Debug vsim DMI vector register support

content.text:
Perfect. Now let me compile all my findings. Let me create a comprehensive response with all the required information:

## FINAL REPORT

### Question 1: Phase 5 section from hybrid_plan.md

**Lines 210-223 (verbatim Phase 5 header and bullet list):**

```
### Phase 5 - Hardening

Each item independently scoped:
- **Vector regfile transfer**: measure DM cost; if >100ms per handoff, add mmap-shared V regfile via DPI (mirror the `set_ilm()` pattern in `src/cpu_wrappers/ax45mpv_premium_cpu_cluster_subsystem.hpp:393-441`).
- **FP regs and `mstatus.FS`**: standard handling.
- **PMP**: all 64 pmpaddr/pmpcfg via Abstract Commands.
- **Priv-mode transitions**: set DCSR.prv so `dret` resumes in the right mode.
- **Traps inside ROI**: trap stays in vsim until exit-CSR retires.
- **MMIO touch in ROI**: add an interconnect watch (`src/platform/interconnect.hpp`), fail loudly on non-RAM addresses during the ROI window.
- **mcycle/minstret discontinuity**: sum across handoffs to keep monotonic; document the gap vs. fully-RTL.
- **lr/sc reservations**: explicitly invalidate via DCSR after resume.
- **Multi-hart**: ABI struct already carries per-hart arrays; `_NDS_NHART <= 2` already enforced (`src/cpu_wrappers/ax45mpv_premium_cpu_cluster_subsystem.hpp:37-39`).

Test: torture workload running interrupt-driven code, V+FP active, traps inside ROI, verifying mcycle/minstret monotonic across multiple handoffs.
```

**Other relevant lines mentioning vector regfile, DPI, Program Buffer, set_ilm, or >100ms:**

- **Line 67**: "Vector registers and any CPU-specific custom CSR that DM doesn't expose are deferred to Phase 5. If DM coverage is incomplete we add a per-CPU Program Buffer fallback or, last resort, an mmap-shared V regfile via DPI."
- **Line 141**: "Risk to escalate: if DM Abstract Commands cannot reach vector regs / Andes-custom CSRs, Phase 5 must add Program Buffer or DPI - decide before starting Phase 2."
- **Line 213**: (main Phase 5 bullet) "**Vector regfile transfer**: measure DM cost; if >100ms per handoff, add mmap-shared V regfile via DPI (mirror the `set_ilm()` pattern in `src/cpu_wrappers/ax45mpv_premium_cpu_cluster_subsystem.hpp:393-441`)."

---

### Question 2: Vector instruction encoders in verilator/src and verilator/tests

**ONLY TWO vector-instruction encoders exist, both in the Program Buffer infrastructure:**

1. **`encode_vsetvl(rd, rs1, rs2)`** at `/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/program_buffer.hpp:46-54`
   - Encodes the RVV `vsetvl rd, rs1, rs2` (R-form, funct7=0b1000000, opcode 0x57)
   - Used in `/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/resume_driver.hpp:97`

2. **`encode_csrrw(rd, csr, rs1)`** at `/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/program_buffer.hpp:59-66`
   - Encodes a CSR write instruction for Program Buffer fallback (e.g., writing unreachable custom CSRs)
   - Used in `/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/resume_driver.hpp:160`

**NO encoders for `vle8.v`, `vse8.v`, or `vsetvli` (I-form) exist in the codebase.** The vector register transport is handled entirely at the C++ application level (memcpy into/out of scratch RAM) in `/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/v_regfile_xfer.hpp:76-93`. Fixtures execute the `vle8.v`/`vse8.v` instructions directly; they are not synthesized by C++ code.

**`regno_vector(i)` usage:**
- **Defined** at `/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/abstract_command.hpp:17` as `constexpr auto regno_vector(uint16_t i) -> uint16_t { return 0x1040u + i; }`
- **ONLY used in unit tests** at `/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/tests/hybrid/abstract_command.test.cpp:14,24-25` to verify the encoding maps v0..v31 to DMI register numbers 0x1040..0x105F
- **NEVER CALLED in actual driver code** — `resume_driver.hpp` does not write vector registers via Abstract Commands; Phase 5 is explicitly deferred because the DM may not support vector register access.

---

### Question 3: Debug Module / Program Buffer size configuration in vsim

**The Debug Module progbuf size is NOT queried at runtime; it is hardcoded:**

- **`kProgbufMaxInsns = 15`** at `/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/program_buffer.hpp:28`
  - This is the maximum number of user instructions (payload); the 16th slot is reserved for the mandatory `ebreak` that terminates execution.
  - Total progbuf size assumed to be 16 x 32-bit words = 64 bytes.
  - No code confirms the AX45MPV DM actually has >=2 usable slots; **this is a risk** that Phase 0 mitigates via `test_dm_coverage.tcl` (hybrid_plan.md line 137).

**No `progbufsize` CSR reading exists.** The abstraction is duck-typed: the `exec_progbuf()` function (line 76) throws `std::runtime_error` if `insns.size() > kProgbufMaxInsns` but never queries the actual hardware limit. The assumption is baked in.

**DMI interface for progbuf:**
- Progbuf registers are accessed via `kProgbuf0 = 0x20` (first register address), with subsequent instructions at 0x21, 0x22, etc.
- The Abstract Command register (abstractcs) at DMI addr 0x16 controls execution via `kExecProgbufCmd = (aarsize << 20) | (1 << 18)` (postexec=1, transfer=0, cmdtype=AccessRegister).
- No explicit inquiry into `abstractcs.progbufsize` (bits [15:12]); the code assumes 15 usable slots.

**How the Debug Module is exposed in vsim:**
- `/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/systemc_dmi_bus.hpp:14-47` is a SystemC adapter that wraps the AHB bus and provides duck-typed `write(uint8_t reg, uint32_t val)` and `read(uint8_t reg) -> uint32_t` methods matching the Debug Module's DMI register interface.
- The actual JTAG DTM and DM logic lives inside the Verilator RTL (Andes ax45mpv_premium CPU); vsim talks to it via AHB-based DMI address/data transactions.

---

### Question 4: dcsr.stopcount / debug-mode counter behavior

**No dcsr (CSR 0x7B0) register is referenced anywhere in the codebase:**
- Grep for `dcsr`, `0x7b0`, `stopcount`, `debug.mode`, or counter-related halt behavior yields **zero results** in verilator/src and verilator/tests.

**What IS recorded about counters:**
- `/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/resume_driver.hpp:113-119` explicitly saves and restores `mcycle` and `minstret` via Abstract Commands so the vsim phase continues counting from QEMU1's drain value.
- The comment (line 114-115) states the intention: "Without this, vsim's local counter starts at zero and any cycles spent inside the ROI vanish at the QEMU2 boundary."
- `/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/state_drain.hpp` reads both counters back before exiting vsim.

**Implication:** The design assumes that executing instructions via the Program Buffer **DOES increment mcycle/minstret** (counters are not frozen while in debug mode). This assumption is documented as a concern but not verified in the current Phase.

---

### Question 5: set_ilm DPI backdoor pattern

**Location and mechanism:**
- **File:** `/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/cpu_wrappers/ax45mpv_premium_cpu_cluster_subsystem.hpp:393-441`
- **Function:** `void set_ilm(const std::vector<uint8_t>& data)` (lines 393-441)

**What it does (2-3 line summary):**
The `set_ilm()` method directly accesses the Verilator RTL's internal ILM (Instruction Local Memory) array via the CPU wrapper's `rootp` pointer (direct Verilator module hierarchy traversal), bypassing the normal SystemC bus. It loops through the input byte vector, packs words with ECC/parity codes, and writes them directly into the RTL memory array. This enables fast in-process state mirroring without DMI transactions—exactly what Phase 5 proposes for vector registers: an mmap-shared V regfile with similar direct-access semantics instead of slow Abstract Command loops.

---

**SUMMARY FOR PHASE 5 PLANNING:**

1. **Vector register transport today** (lines 213, v_regfile_xfer.hpp): fixture-aware scratch RAM approach; no hardware/DPI support yet.
2. **The >100ms threshold** (line 213): triggers DPI path if Abstract Command loop takes >100ms per handoff.
3. **The set_ilm precedent** (line 393-441): proof-of-concept that direct Verilator RTL access via CPU wrapper is feasible; Phase 5 can adapt this for V regfile mirroring.
4. **No dcsr handling** yet; mcycle/minstret **are** preserved across handoffs, confirming counter behavior during Program Buffer execution is expected to work.
5. **Program Buffer assumption** (kProgbufMaxInsns=15) is hardcoded; Phase 0's `test_dm_coverage.tcl` is meant to validate the AX45MPV DM before committing to this limit.

aiTitle: Debug vsim DMI vector register support

content.text:
Both agents confirm the mechanism. Key findings that shape the plan:

- **Phase 5 doc** lists *both* PB and DPI for V-transfer; PB is the stated fallback (`hybrid_plan.md:67,141`), DPI the >100ms-gated primary (`:213`). User chose PB - it reuses existing infra and needs no RTL backdoor.
- **Only** `encode_vsetvl`/`encode_csrrw` exist; no `vle8.v`/`vse8.v` encoders. `regno_vector` exists but is used *only in a test* - confirms abstract-command V access was never wired, PB is the path.
- **No `dcsr.stopcount` anywhere** - so PB transport insns *would* hit `mcycle`/`minstret` unless I order around them. This directly answers your earlier "does PB affect vsim cycles" worry, and the fix is pure ordering (no new hardware assumption).
- Test idiom is clear: scan `bus.ops` for a `kProgbuf0` write equal to the encoded insn, with DATA0/DATA1 GPR-staging writes preceding it.

Let me run one Plan agent to stress-test the riskiest leaf nodes - the clobber/ordering correctness and the exact instruction encodings - before I finalize.

input.prompt:
Validate a design for moving RISC-V vector-register transport into a simulator's Debug Module Program Buffer (PB). This is read-only validation - find correctness bugs in my ordering/clobber logic and confirm exact instruction encodings. Repo: /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim.

BACKGROUND - the hybrid co-simulator does a 3-phase handoff QEMU1 (drain) -> vsim (resume+run+drain) -> QEMU2 (restore). V registers currently round-trip through a guest-RAM scratch region at GPA 0x00FF0000 (constant kVScratchGpa in verilator/src/hybrid/v_regfile_xfer.hpp). TODAY the FIXTURE binary issues the vle8.v/vse8.v sweep (tests/fixtures/runtime/v_regfile_xfer.c) in M-mode. GOAL: move that sweep into vsim, executed via the Debug Module Program Buffer while the hart is halted, so benchmark binaries need no transport asm.

Read these files first:
- verilator/src/hybrid/resume_driver.hpp (ResumeDriver::resume - note existing Tier-B vsetvl PB use at lines 90-104, mcycle/minstret restore lines 113-119, write_64bit_register, write_csr_abstract_or_pb)
- verilator/src/hybrid/state_drain.hpp (StateDrain::drain and drain_with_passthrough - V-CSR reads lines 71-78, mcycle/minstret reads lines 80-81)
- verilator/src/hybrid/program_buffer.hpp (exec_progbuf, encode_vsetvl R-form, kProgbuf0=0x20, kProgbufMaxInsns=15)
- verilator/src/hybrid/abstract_command.hpp (regno_gpr, access_register_command)
- verilator/src/hybrid/v_regfile_xfer.hpp (kVScratchGpa=0x00FF0000, kVScratchBytes=32*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX, stage_v_to_scratch, read_v_from_scratch)
- verilator/src/simulator.hpp lines 198-216 (apply_resume_from_qemu: calls driver.resume() THEN stage_v_to_scratch) and lines 488-513 (drain path: on_retire then read_v_from_scratch)
- include/hybrid/state_abi.h (hybrid_state_v1: vreg[], vlenb, vtype, vl, vstart fields; HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX; HYBRID_FLAG_V)

MY PROPOSED DESIGN - validate each claim:

RESUME side (inside ResumeDriver::resume, HYBRID_FLAG_V branch):
1. simulator.hpp must call stage_v_to_scratch BEFORE driver.resume() (currently it's AFTER). The PB vle8.v reads guest RAM, so scratch must be populated first. CONFIRM this reordering is correct and that ResumeDriver itself needs NO RAM access (only the kVScratchGpa constant + state.vlenb + DMI bus).
2. New PB reload sweep, inserted AFTER mstatus.VS enable but BEFORE the existing architectural V-CSR restore (the vsetvl/vstart/vxsat block at lines 90-104):
   a. abstract-write x5 = state.vlenb (avl), x6 = 0xC0 (vtype for e8,m1,ta,ma)
   b. exec_progbuf {encode_vsetvl(0,5,6)} -> sets vtype=e8m1, vl=vlenb, vstart=0
   c. for i in 0..31: abstract-write x7 = kVScratchGpa + i*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX; exec_progbuf {encode_vle8_v(i, 7)}
   This clobbers vtype/vl/vstart and x5/x6/x7.
3. The EXISTING architectural V-CSR restore (vsetvl from state.vl/state.vtype, then vstart/vxsat/vxrm/vcsr) runs AFTER step 2, overwriting the sweep's vtype/vl/vstart. CONFIRM this ordering makes the final architectural vtype/vl/vstart correct.
4. GPR loop x1..x31 runs after, overwriting x5/x6/x7. CONFIRM no leak.
5. mcycle/minstret restored to exact QEMU1 values at the END (lines 113-119), AFTER the sweep, so the 32 vle8.v + vsetvl PB executions cannot pollute the resumed counters. CONFIRM.

DRAIN side (inside StateDrain::drain_with_passthrough, HYBRID_FLAG_V branch):
6. ALL reads happen first: drain() reads x1..x31+pc, then FPR, then V-CSRs (vstart/vtype/vl/...), then mcycle/minstret. THEN a NEW PB dump sweep runs LAST: vsetvl e8m1 + 32x (abstract-write x7=base; exec vse8.v vN,(x7)). Because counters and V-CSRs were already captured, the sweep's clobbers and counter increments don't corrupt the drained state. CONFIRM ordering: is reading GPRs before clobbering x5/x6/x7 sufficient given QEMU2 restores all GPRs from state.gpr[] anyway?

ENCODINGS - verify these bit patterns against the RVV 1.0 spec:
- vtype for e8,m1,ta,ma == 0xC0 (vma bit7=1, vta bit6=1, vsew[5:3]=0, vlmul[2:0]=0)?
- vle8.v vd,(rs1) == (1u<<25)|(rs1<<15)|(vd<<7)|0x07  (vm=1, width=000 for EEW8, mop=00 unit-stride, mew=0, nf=000, lumop=00000, opcode LOAD-FP=0x07)?
- vse8.v vs3,(rs1) == (1u<<25)|(rs1<<15)|(vs3<<7)|0x27 (sumop=00000, opcode STORE-FP=0x27)?
Report the correct hex for vle8.v v3,(x7) and vse8.v v3,(x7) as concrete examples.

ALSO ANALYZE:
7. Scratch layout: stage_v_to_scratch memcpy's the full kVScratchBytes (32*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX=2048) contiguously, vreg[i] at offset i*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX(=64). The PB sweep uses per-reg base stride HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX(64) but loads only vlenb bytes (vl=vlenb). For VLEN=512 vlenb=64 so stride==load-count. CONFIRM the PB pointer stride must be HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX (NOT vlenb) to match the staged layout, and that loading only vlenb bytes per reg is correct.
8. PB size: each sweep PB execution is 1 instruction (vle8.v or vse8.v), well under kProgbufMaxInsns=15. CONFIRM no batching needed for correctness.
9. COMPILER BARRIER RISK: removing the fixture's v_regfile_reload_all/v_regfile_dump_all opaque calls - does this risk the compiler hoisting phase-2 vector COMPUTE outside the enter_vsim/exit_vsim markers (which are asm volatile with "memory" clobber)? Read tests/fixtures/runtime/rt_c_helpers.h and tests/fixtures/rt_c_v_regs.c. Assess: for the CORRECTNESS test (asserts out[]==expected) does it matter if all 32 V regs round-trip regardless of which phase computed y? Separately, for a realistic cycle-measuring benchmark, would losing the opaque-call barrier let the kernel insns drift out of the ROI window? Recommend whether enter_vsim/exit_vsim need strengthening into out-of-line (non-inline, separate-TU) functions, or whether the existing markers + the "vr" force-live guards suffice.

Deliver: a point-by-point verdict (CORRECT / BUG: ...) for items 1-9, the concrete encoder hex values, and any ordering bug or missed clobber I should fix. Do NOT write any code or files - analysis only.

aiTitle: Debug vsim DMI vector register support

content.text:
Write is not available in this read-only context. That's fine - the instructions say to return findings directly as my final message, not write files. Here is the complete validation.

---

# V-register PB transport: point-by-point validation

## RESUME side

### Item 1 - reorder `stage_v_to_scratch` before `driver.resume()`: CORRECT

Confirmed. Today `simulator.hpp:204-215` calls `driver.resume(...)` first, then `stage_v_to_scratch`. Once the PB `vle8.v` sweep lives inside `resume()`, the loads read guest RAM at `kVScratchGpa`, so scratch MUST be populated first. The reorder is correct and required.

`ResumeDriver` needs NO RAM access: confirmed. It only uses the `kVScratchGpa` constant (a fixed GPA literal it pokes into x7 via abstract write), `state.vlenb`, and the `DmiBus`. The `vle8.v` instruction itself does the RAM read inside the halted hart's memory pipe, not through `ResumeDriver`. The `v_regfile_xfer.hpp` `stage_v_to_scratch` helper (which does touch `platform.main_memory`) stays in `simulator.hpp`, not in the driver. Clean separation.

### Item 2 - new PB reload sweep after mstatus.VS, before the architectural V-CSR block: CORRECT, but with a **clobber-ordering BUG against the existing Tier-B block**

Your step 2 inserts the reload sweep *before* the existing block at lines 90-104. But the existing block at lines 95-98 ALSO stages x5=`state.vl`, x6=`state.vtype` and runs `encode_vsetvl(0,5,6)`. Sequence as you describe it:

```
[2a] x5 = vlenb,  x6 = 0xC0
[2b] vsetvl x0,x5,x6        -> vtype=e8m1, vl=vlenb
[2c] loop: x7 = base; vle8.v vN,(x7)     (32x)
[3 ] x5 = state.vl, x6 = state.vtype     (existing lines 95-96)
     vsetvl x0,x5,x6                      (existing line 97)  -> architectural vtype/vl
     vstart/vxsat/vxrm/vcsr restore       (existing lines 100-103)
```

This is actually **correct in result** because step 3 re-stages x5/x6 and re-runs vsetvl, overwriting the sweep's e8m1 vl/vtype with the architectural values. The two `vsetvl` PB executions are independent. No real collision - just redundant scratch writes, which is fine.

**One real subtlety to confirm (BUG class: latent):** your reload sweep clobbers x5/x6/x7. Today the code comment at line 88-94 promises "the GPR loop overwrites x5 afterward so state is preserved." That invariant still holds because the GPR loop (lines 107-108) runs after BOTH the sweep and the existing V-CSR block. But you now also clobber **x7**, which the existing comments do not mention. The GPR loop writes x1..x31 including x7, so x7 is restored. No leak - but update the comment, x7 joins x5/x6 as scratch.

**vsetvl AVL semantics check (important):** for e8/m1 at VLEN=512, VLMAX = VLEN/SEW × LMUL = 512/8 × 1 = 64 = vlenb. Setting AVL=x5=vlenb=64 gives vl=min(AVL, VLMAX)=64. So `vl == vlenb`, and the sweep loads exactly vlenb bytes per register. Correct. If a future config had VLEN where vlenb > VLMAX for e8m1 this could not happen (e8m1 always has VLMAX==vlenb), so this is robust by construction for e8/m1.

### Item 3 - architectural V-CSR restore after the sweep overwrites sweep's vtype/vl/vstart: CORRECT

The existing block (lines 95-104) runs after your sweep and sets the final architectural vtype/vl (via `vsetvl x0,x5,x6` from `state.vl`/`state.vtype`) then `vstart`. Final vtype/vl/vstart are the QEMU1 architectural values, not the sweep's e8m1/64/0. Correct.

**Sharp edge worth flagging:** the architectural `vsetvl` at line 97 computes `vl = min(state.vl, VLMAX(state.vtype))`. If `state.vtype` encodes a different SEW/LMUL than e8m1 and `state.vl` was a value that is legal under QEMU's vtype, the AX45MPV vsetvl will reproduce the same vl as long as the hart's VLEN matches QEMU's. This is a pre-existing property of the Tier-B path, not introduced by your change. Not a new bug.

### Item 4 - GPR loop overwrites x5/x6/x7, no leak: CORRECT

GPR loop (lines 107-108) writes x1..x31. x5, x6, x7 all restored from `state.gpr[]`. No scratch leak into the resumed context.

### Item 5 - mcycle/minstret restored at the END after the sweep: CORRECT

Lines 113-119 restore `state.mcycle`/`state.minstret` last, after the sweep. The 32 `vle8.v` + 2 `vsetvl` PB executions increment the hart's hardware counters during halt-mode execution, but those increments are overwritten by the final abstract/PB CSR writes. Resumed counters equal QEMU1's drain values exactly. Correct.

**Caveat (not a bug, a hardware assumption):** this assumes the AX45MPV increments mcycle/minstret during Program Buffer execution at all, OR does not. Either way you overwrite at the end, so it does not matter - which is precisely why doing it last is the right design. Confirmed robust.

## DRAIN side

### Item 6 - all reads first, then PB dump sweep last: CORRECT

`drain_with_passthrough` reads in order: GPRs+pc (via `drain()`), FPRs, then the six V-CSRs (vstart/vxsat/vxrm/vcsr/vtype/vl, lines 71-78), then mcycle/minstret (lines 80-81). Your new dump sweep runs *after* all of these. Because every captured field is already in `state`, the sweep's clobbers (x5/x6/x7, vtype/vl/vstart) and its counter increments cannot corrupt drained state.

Your specific question - "is reading GPRs before clobbering x5/x6/x7 sufficient given QEMU2 restores all GPRs from state.gpr[] anyway?": **CORRECT, and even stronger than you state.** Two independent reasons the clobber is harmless:
1. GPRs x1..x31 are read into `state.gpr[]` by `drain()` *before* the sweep runs, so the captured values are pre-clobber.
2. Even if (1) were not true, QEMU2's `ResumeDriver`/gdbstub restores all 32 GPRs from `state.gpr[]`, so a live-hart clobber of x5/x6/x7 after capture is invisible.

Either reason alone suffices. Ordering is correct.

### Item 6 - **BUG: vstart vs the dump sweep's vse8.v**

This is the one genuine correctness bug across the whole design, on the drain side.

`vse8.v vN,(x7)` is a unit-stride store that begins at element index `vstart` and stores elements `[vstart, vl)`. If the drained hart has `vstart != 0` at the exit trigger, then:

- You read `state.vstart` first (line 72) - good, the value is captured.
- But then your dump sweep does `vsetvl x0,x5,x6` (e8m1, vl=vlenb). **`vsetvl` resets vstart to 0** (RVV spec: every vset{i}vl{i} sets vstart=0). So by the time `vse8.v` runs, vstart is already 0 and the store covers all elements `[0, vlenb)`. The full register is dumped.

So in practice the sweep's own `vsetvl` saves you: vstart is forced to 0 before any `vse8.v`. **The dump is complete.** Confirmed not a bug *as long as the dump sweep issues `vsetvl` before the first `vse8.v`* (which your design does in step "vsetvl e8m1 + 32x vse8.v").

The symmetric concern on RESUME: after your reload sweep, `vle8.v` with vstart=0 (vsetvl reset it) loads all elements. Then the architectural `vstart` restore (line 100) sets `state.vstart` for the resumed program. So a non-zero architectural vstart is preserved into QEMU2's view. Correct.

**Net:** vstart handling is correct *because* both sweeps lead with vsetvl. If you ever drop the per-sweep vsetvl (e.g. "vtype is already e8m1 from a prior op, skip vsetvl"), you reintroduce a partial-store/partial-load bug whenever vstart != 0. Keep the vsetvl in every sweep. Document this dependency.

### Drain side - missing symmetric reorder (gap, not in your 9 items)

You did not mention it, but the icount path is symmetric. `simulator.hpp:492-493` dispatches to either `hybrid_handoff_` or `hybrid_icount_handoff_`, both of which call `drain_with_passthrough` via `HandoffController::on_retire` (handoff_controller.hpp:63). Moving the dump sweep *into* `StateDrain::drain_with_passthrough` (header-only, shared by both controllers) covers both paths in one place. Today the sweep is in the fixture, and `read_v_from_scratch` runs in `simulator.hpp:500-506` (`check_hybrid_handoff`) for both. After your change, `read_v_from_scratch` should still run there (the DM `vse8.v` lands bytes in RAM that `read_v_from_scratch` then copies into `state.vreg[]`). So the data flow becomes: PB `vse8.v` (inside `StateDrain`) -> RAM scratch -> `read_v_from_scratch` (in `simulator.hpp`) -> `state.vreg[]`. Confirm you keep `read_v_from_scratch` in place; only the asm source of the bytes moves from fixture to PB.

## ENCODINGS (verified against RVV 1.0)

All computed and confirmed:

| Encoding | Value | Check |
|---|---|---|
| vtype e8,m1,ta,ma | `0xC0` | vma(b7)=1, vta(b6)=1, vsew[5:3]=000, vlmul[2:0]=000. CORRECT. |
| vle8.v vd,(rs1) formula | `(1<<25)|(rs1<<15)|(vd<<7)|0x07` | CORRECT. |
| vse8.v vs3,(rs1) formula | `(1<<25)|(rs1<<15)|(vs3<<7)|0x27` | CORRECT. |
| **vle8.v v3,(x7)** | **`0x02038187`** | concrete example |
| **vse8.v v3,(x7)** | **`0x020381A7`** | concrete example |
| vle8.v v0,(x7) | `0x02038007` | |
| vse8.v v0,(x7) | `0x02038027` | |
| vle8.v v31,(x7) | `0x02038F87` | |
| vse8.v v31,(x7) | `0x02038FA7` | |
| vsetvl x0,x5,x6 | `0x8062F057` | matches existing `encode_vsetvl(0,5,6)` already in tree |

Cross-check: the fixture today uses I-form `vsetvli zero,a4,e8,m1,ta,ma` which encodes to `0xC077057` with zimm=`0xC0` (a4=x14). That confirms the `0xC0` vtype field is consistent between the I-form your fixture uses and the R-form `vsetvl` your PB design uses. The R-form takes vtype from x6 instead of an immediate, but the bit field is identical (`0xC0`).

**Field detail confirmed for vle8/vse8:** bit25=vm=1 (unmasked), bits[14:12]=width=000 (EEW=8), bit[14:12] mew=0, bits[31:29] nf=000, bits[27:26] mop=00 (unit-stride), bits[24:20] lumop/sumop=00000. The single bit you set at `1<<25` is `vm=1`. The full upper field `mew|nf|...` is all zero, so `1<<25` correctly produces the unmasked unit-stride form. CORRECT. (Note: `vle8.v` and `vse8.v` differ only in the opcode `0x07` vs `0x27`, i.e. LOAD-FP vs STORE-FP. CORRECT.)

## Item 7 - scratch layout / stride: CORRECT

`stage_v_to_scratch` (v_regfile_xfer.hpp:76-82) does one contiguous `memcpy` of `kVScratchBytes = 32 * HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX = 2048` bytes from `state.vreg`. `state.vreg` itself is declared `uint8_t vreg[32 * HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX]` (state_abi.h:76), so register i sits at offset `i * HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX = i*64`.

Therefore the PB pointer stride MUST be `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX` (64), **NOT** vlenb. Your design says stride=64, load-count=vl=vlenb. For VLEN=512, vlenb=64=stride, so they coincide and any choice "happens to work." But the *correct* stride is `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX` because that is the staging stride. CONFIRMED: stride=`HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX`, load `vlenb` bytes per register.

If you ever build with VLEN < 512 (vlenb < 64), the wire format still reserves 64 bytes/reg (the ABI is sized to max). Using stride=vlenb in the PB would then read register i's bytes from the wrong offset (i*vlenb instead of i*64) and desync from the staged layout. **Stride must be the compile-time `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX` constant, never the runtime vlenb.** This matches what the fixture does today (`addi t0, t0, 64` - a hardcoded 64, which equals `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX`).

Loading only vlenb bytes (vl=vlenb via vsetvl e8m1) is correct: vlenb is the true per-register byte count; bytes [vlenb, 64) in each 64-byte slot are padding when VLEN<512 and need not be loaded/stored.

## Item 8 - PB size: CORRECT

Each sweep PB execution is exactly 1 payload instruction (`vle8.v` or `vse8.v`); `exec_progbuf` appends the mandatory `kEbreak`, so 2 slots used out of 16. Far under `kProgbufMaxInsns=15`. No batching needed for correctness.

**Optimization note (not correctness):** you issue 32 separate `exec_progbuf` calls plus 32 abstract x7 writes plus 32 busy-polls per sweep. You *could* batch a few `vle8.v`+`addi` pairs into one PB exec (up to 7 load+addi pairs fit in 15 slots), cutting the number of `exec_progbuf` round-trips ~7x. Not required for correctness; flag for perf if the resume/drain DMI latency matters. The current per-reg-with-abstract-x7-write design is the simplest correct form.

## Item 9 - compiler barrier / ROI hoisting: existing markers SUFFICIENT for correctness; recommend strengthening for benchmarks

Read `rt_c_helpers.h` and `rt_c_v_regs.c`. Analysis:

**For the CORRECTNESS test (asserts `out[i]==expected`):** the test does NOT care which phase computed `y`. The assertions check the closed-form FIR result. Three guarantees keep it correct regardless of barrier removal:
1. `enter_vsim`/`exit_vsim` are `asm volatile(... ::: "memory")` - they block load/store reordering across the marker and force globals to spill/reload.
2. The `asm volatile("" : : "vr"(x))` (line 58) and `"vr"(y)` (line 73) force-live guards pin `x` before `enter_vsim` and `y` before `exit_vsim` into V registers at those exact source points. The compiler cannot dead-code `x`'s phase-1 compute (it is observed by the `vr` asm) and cannot sink `y`'s compute past the `vr(y)` guard.
3. Once the PB sweep round-trips **all 32** V regs bit-identically (just as the fixture's `v_regfile_*_all` did), the compiler's "x lives in v_compiler_choice / y lives in v_compiler_choice" tracking stays valid across the boundary. Whichever physical reg the compiler picked for x and y is preserved by the full-regfile sweep.

So removing `v_regfile_reload_all`/`v_regfile_dump_all` from the fixture does NOT break the correctness test, provided the PB sweep is a faithful full-regfile round-trip. The opaque-call barrier those functions provided is replaced by: (a) the `"memory"` clobber on `enter_vsim`/`exit_vsim`, plus (b) the `"vr"` force-live guards on x and y.

**BUT there is a residual hoisting risk for x and the FIR compute:** the `v_regfile_reload_all()` call sat between `enter_vsim()` and the phase-2 compute (line 61). Being an out-of-line function with a `"memory"` clobber, it was a second barrier *inside* the ROI. Removing it leaves only `enter_vsim()` (also `"memory"`-clobbered) before the FIR. The FIR inputs (`x`) are pinned by `vr(x)` at line 58 *before* `enter_vsim`. The compiler is free to *re-materialize* the FIR compute anywhere after `x` is available, including speculatively before `enter_vsim`, because the FIR (slide/add) instructions are pure (no memory, no volatile) and are not themselves observed until `vr(y)`. The `"memory"` clobber on `enter_vsim` does NOT order pure vector arithmetic - it only orders memory operations. So:

- **Correctness:** still fine. Even if the FIR drifts before `enter_vsim`, the final `y` is correct, and the full-regfile sweep carries whatever V regs are live across the boundary. The `out[]==expected` assertions pass.
- **Benchmark / cycle-accuracy:** **NOT fine.** For a realistic cycle-measuring benchmark, the whole point is that the FIR kernel insns execute *on vsim*, inside the `enter_vsim`/`exit_vsim` window, so their cycles are counted by mcycle. If the compiler hoists the pure vector compute before `enter_vsim` (computed on QEMU1) or sinks it after `exit_vsim` (computed on QEMU2), the kernel's cycles vanish from the ROI. The `"vr"` guards pin the *values* at source points but do not force the *producing instructions* to stay between the markers - the compiler may recompute. The lost out-of-line barrier made hoisting much harder (a real call the optimizer would not speculate across); inline `asm volatile("memory")` markers do not constrain pure RVV arithmetic motion.

**Recommendation:** for benchmark binaries that measure ROI cycles, strengthen `enter_vsim`/`exit_vsim` into **out-of-line, separate-TU, non-inline** functions (declared in a header, defined in their own `.c`, compiled without LTO cross-inlining of the marker body, or marked `__attribute__((noinline,noipa))`). A real call boundary the optimizer cannot see through prevents the pure vector kernel from migrating out of the ROI window. The current inline `asm volatile` + `"vr"` guards are sufficient ONLY for the functional correctness fixture, not for cycle attribution.

For the correctness test specifically (your immediate goal), the existing markers + `"vr"` guards suffice; you can drop the `v_regfile_*_all` calls. For any cycle-measuring use, move the markers out of line.

## Summary table

| Item | Verdict |
|---|---|
| 1 | CORRECT - reorder required; driver needs no RAM access |
| 2 | CORRECT - add x7 to the documented scratch-clobber set; redundant-but-safe double vsetvl |
| 3 | CORRECT |
| 4 | CORRECT |
| 5 | CORRECT |
| 6 | CORRECT - clobber harmless for two independent reasons |
| 6 (vstart) | CORRECT only because each sweep leads with vsetvl (which forces vstart=0). Keep the vsetvl in every sweep or partial load/store reappears when vstart!=0 |
| 7 | CORRECT - stride must be compile-time HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX, never runtime vlenb |
| 8 | CORRECT - 1 insn/exec, no batching needed |
| 9 | Correctness test: markers + "vr" guards SUFFICIENT. Benchmark cycle-accuracy: NOT sufficient, move markers out-of-line |

## Bugs / fixes to apply

1. **Keep `vsetvl` in every PB sweep** (resume reload and drain dump). It is what forces vstart=0 before the vle8.v/vse8.v unit-stride access. Dropping it (e.g. as a perf "optimization" when vtype is already e8m1) reintroduces a partial-element load/store bug whenever the drained hart has vstart != 0. This is the single subtle correctness trap in the design.
2. **Stride = `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX` (64), not vlenb.** Use the compile-time constant for the x7 increment to match the staged layout. Loading vlenb bytes per reg is correct.
3. **Document x7 as scratch.** The existing comments (resume_driver.hpp lines 88-94, 117) promise only x5/x6 are clobbered-then-restored. Your sweep adds x7. The GPR loop restores it, so no leak, but the comment is now stale.
4. **Put the dump sweep inside `StateDrain::drain_with_passthrough`** (header-only, shared by both `hybrid_handoff_` and `hybrid_icount_handoff_`) so both trigger paths get it. Keep `read_v_from_scratch` in `simulator.hpp:500-506` - the PB `vse8.v` writes RAM, then `read_v_from_scratch` copies RAM into `state.vreg[]`. Symmetrically, the reload sweep goes inside `ResumeDriver::resume`, and `stage_v_to_scratch` stays in `simulator.hpp` but moves to *before* `driver.resume()`.

### Critical Files for Implementation
- /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/resume_driver.hpp
- /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/state_drain.hpp
- /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/program_buffer.hpp
- /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/simulator.hpp
- /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/tests/fixtures/runtime/v_regfile_xfer.c (the asm being retired; rt_c_v_regs.c will drop the two `v_regfile_*_all` calls)

toolUseResult.prompt:
Validate a design for moving RISC-V vector-register transport into a simulator's Debug Module Program Buffer (PB). This is read-only validation - find correctness bugs in my ordering/clobber logic and confirm exact instruction encodings. Repo: /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim.

BACKGROUND - the hybrid co-simulator does a 3-phase handoff QEMU1 (drain) -> vsim (resume+run+drain) -> QEMU2 (restore). V registers currently round-trip through a guest-RAM scratch region at GPA 0x00FF0000 (constant kVScratchGpa in verilator/src/hybrid/v_regfile_xfer.hpp). TODAY the FIXTURE binary issues the vle8.v/vse8.v sweep (tests/fixtures/runtime/v_regfile_xfer.c) in M-mode. GOAL: move that sweep into vsim, executed via the Debug Module Program Buffer while the hart is halted, so benchmark binaries need no transport asm.

Read these files first:
- verilator/src/hybrid/resume_driver.hpp (ResumeDriver::resume - note existing Tier-B vsetvl PB use at lines 90-104, mcycle/minstret restore lines 113-119, write_64bit_register, write_csr_abstract_or_pb)
- verilator/src/hybrid/state_drain.hpp (StateDrain::drain and drain_with_passthrough - V-CSR reads lines 71-78, mcycle/minstret reads lines 80-81)
- verilator/src/hybrid/program_buffer.hpp (exec_progbuf, encode_vsetvl R-form, kProgbuf0=0x20, kProgbufMaxInsns=15)
- verilator/src/hybrid/abstract_command.hpp (regno_gpr, access_register_command)
- verilator/src/hybrid/v_regfile_xfer.hpp (kVScratchGpa=0x00FF0000, kVScratchBytes=32*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX, stage_v_to_scratch, read_v_from_scratch)
- verilator/src/simulator.hpp lines 198-216 (apply_resume_from_qemu: calls driver.resume() THEN stage_v_to_scratch) and lines 488-513 (drain path: on_retire then read_v_from_scratch)
- include/hybrid/state_abi.h (hybrid_state_v1: vreg[], vlenb, vtype, vl, vstart fields; HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX; HYBRID_FLAG_V)

MY PROPOSED DESIGN - validate each claim:

RESUME side (inside ResumeDriver::resume, HYBRID_FLAG_V branch):
1. simulator.hpp must call stage_v_to_scratch BEFORE driver.resume() (currently it's AFTER). The PB vle8.v reads guest RAM, so scratch must be populated first. CONFIRM this reordering is correct and that ResumeDriver itself needs NO RAM access (only the kVScratchGpa constant + state.vlenb + DMI bus).
2. New PB reload sweep, inserted AFTER mstatus.VS enable but BEFORE the existing architectural V-CSR restore (the vsetvl/vstart/vxsat block at lines 90-104):
   a. abstract-write x5 = state.vlenb (avl), x6 = 0xC0 (vtype for e8,m1,ta,ma)
   b. exec_progbuf {encode_vsetvl(0,5,6)} -> sets vtype=e8m1, vl=vlenb, vstart=0
   c. for i in 0..31: abstract-write x7 = kVScratchGpa + i*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX; exec_progbuf {encode_vle8_v(i, 7)}
   This clobbers vtype/vl/vstart and x5/x6/x7.
3. The EXISTING architectural V-CSR restore (vsetvl from state.vl/state.vtype, then vstart/vxsat/vxrm/vcsr) runs AFTER step 2, overwriting the sweep's vtype/vl/vstart. CONFIRM this ordering makes the final architectural vtype/vl/vstart correct.
4. GPR loop x1..x31 runs after, overwriting x5/x6/x7. CONFIRM no leak.
5. mcycle/minstret restored to exact QEMU1 values at the END (lines 113-119), AFTER the sweep, so the 32 vle8.v + vsetvl PB executions cannot pollute the resumed counters. CONFIRM.

DRAIN side (inside StateDrain::drain_with_passthrough, HYBRID_FLAG_V branch):
6. ALL reads happen first: drain() reads x1..x31+pc, then FPR, then V-CSRs (vstart/vtype/vl/...), then mcycle/minstret. THEN a NEW PB dump sweep runs LAST: vsetvl e8m1 + 32x (abstract-write x7=base; exec vse8.v vN,(x7)). Because counters and V-CSRs were already captured, the sweep's clobbers and counter increments don't corrupt the drained state. CONFIRM ordering: is reading GPRs before clobbering x5/x6/x7 sufficient given QEMU2 restores all GPRs from state.gpr[] anyway?

ENCODINGS - verify these bit patterns against the RVV 1.0 spec:
- vtype for e8,m1,ta,ma == 0xC0 (vma bit7=1, vta bit6=1, vsew[5:3]=0, vlmul[2:0]=0)?
- vle8.v vd,(rs1) == (1u<<25)|(rs1<<15)|(vd<<7)|0x07  (vm=1, width=000 for EEW8, mop=00 unit-stride, mew=0, nf=000, lumop=00000, opcode LOAD-FP=0x07)?
- vse8.v vs3,(rs1) == (1u<<25)|(rs1<<15)|(vs3<<7)|0x27 (sumop=00000, opcode STORE-FP=0x27)?
Report the correct hex for vle8.v v3,(x7) and vse8.v v3,(x7) as concrete examples.

ALSO ANALYZE:
7. Scratch layout: stage_v_to_scratch memcpy's the full kVScratchBytes (32*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX=2048) contiguously, vreg[i] at offset i*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX(=64). The PB sweep uses per-reg base stride HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX(64) but loads only vlenb bytes (vl=vlenb). For VLEN=512 vlenb=64 so stride==load-count. CONFIRM the PB pointer stride must be HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX (NOT vlenb) to match the staged layout, and that loading only vlenb bytes per reg is correct.
8. PB size: each sweep PB execution is 1 instruction (vle8.v or vse8.v), well under kProgbufMaxInsns=15. CONFIRM no batching needed for correctness.
9. COMPILER BARRIER RISK: removing the fixture's v_regfile_reload_all/v_regfile_dump_all opaque calls - does this risk the compiler hoisting phase-2 vector COMPUTE outside the enter_vsim/exit_vsim markers (which are asm volatile with "memory" clobber)? Read tests/fixtures/runtime/rt_c_helpers.h and tests/fixtures/rt_c_v_regs.c. Assess: for the CORRECTNESS test (asserts out[]==expected) does it matter if all 32 V regs round-trip regardless of which phase computed y? Separately, for a realistic cycle-measuring benchmark, would losing the opaque-call barrier let the kernel insns drift out of the ROI window? Recommend whether enter_vsim/exit_vsim need strengthening into out-of-line (non-inline, separate-TU) functions, or whether the existing markers + the "vr" force-live guards suffice.

Deliver: a point-by-point verdict (CORRECT / BUG: ...) for items 1-9, the concrete encoder hex values, and any ordering bug or missed clobber I should fix. Do NOT write any code or files - analysis only.

content.text:
Write is not available in this read-only context. That's fine - the instructions say to return findings directly as my final message, not write files. Here is the complete validation.

---

# V-register PB transport: point-by-point validation

## RESUME side

### Item 1 - reorder `stage_v_to_scratch` before `driver.resume()`: CORRECT

Confirmed. Today `simulator.hpp:204-215` calls `driver.resume(...)` first, then `stage_v_to_scratch`. Once the PB `vle8.v` sweep lives inside `resume()`, the loads read guest RAM at `kVScratchGpa`, so scratch MUST be populated first. The reorder is correct and required.

`ResumeDriver` needs NO RAM access: confirmed. It only uses the `kVScratchGpa` constant (a fixed GPA literal it pokes into x7 via abstract write), `state.vlenb`, and the `DmiBus`. The `vle8.v` instruction itself does the RAM read inside the halted hart's memory pipe, not through `ResumeDriver`. The `v_regfile_xfer.hpp` `stage_v_to_scratch` helper (which does touch `platform.main_memory`) stays in `simulator.hpp`, not in the driver. Clean separation.

### Item 2 - new PB reload sweep after mstatus.VS, before the architectural V-CSR block: CORRECT, but with a **clobber-ordering BUG against the existing Tier-B block**

Your step 2 inserts the reload sweep *before* the existing block at lines 90-104. But the existing block at lines 95-98 ALSO stages x5=`state.vl`, x6=`state.vtype` and runs `encode_vsetvl(0,5,6)`. Sequence as you describe it:

```
[2a] x5 = vlenb,  x6 = 0xC0
[2b] vsetvl x0,x5,x6        -> vtype=e8m1, vl=vlenb
[2c] loop: x7 = base; vle8.v vN,(x7)     (32x)
[3 ] x5 = state.vl, x6 = state.vtype     (existing lines 95-96)
     vsetvl x0,x5,x6                      (existing line 97)  -> architectural vtype/vl
     vstart/vxsat/vxrm/vcsr restore       (existing lines 100-103)
```

This is actually **correct in result** because step 3 re-stages x5/x6 and re-runs vsetvl, overwriting the sweep's e8m1 vl/vtype with the architectural values. The two `vsetvl` PB executions are independent. No real collision - just redundant scratch writes, which is fine.

**One real subtlety to confirm (BUG class: latent):** your reload sweep clobbers x5/x6/x7. Today the code comment at line 88-94 promises "the GPR loop overwrites x5 afterward so state is preserved." That invariant still holds because the GPR loop (lines 107-108) runs after BOTH the sweep and the existing V-CSR block. But you now also clobber **x7**, which the existing comments do not mention. The GPR loop writes x1..x31 including x7, so x7 is restored. No leak - but update the comment, x7 joins x5/x6 as scratch.

**vsetvl AVL semantics check (important):** for e8/m1 at VLEN=512, VLMAX = VLEN/SEW × LMUL = 512/8 × 1 = 64 = vlenb. Setting AVL=x5=vlenb=64 gives vl=min(AVL, VLMAX)=64. So `vl == vlenb`, and the sweep loads exactly vlenb bytes per register. Correct. If a future config had VLEN where vlenb > VLMAX for e8m1 this could not happen (e8m1 always has VLMAX==vlenb), so this is robust by construction for e8/m1.

### Item 3 - architectural V-CSR restore after the sweep overwrites sweep's vtype/vl/vstart: CORRECT

The existing block (lines 95-104) runs after your sweep and sets the final architectural vtype/vl (via `vsetvl x0,x5,x6` from `state.vl`/`state.vtype`) then `vstart`. Final vtype/vl/vstart are the QEMU1 architectural values, not the sweep's e8m1/64/0. Correct.

**Sharp edge worth flagging:** the architectural `vsetvl` at line 97 computes `vl = min(state.vl, VLMAX(state.vtype))`. If `state.vtype` encodes a different SEW/LMUL than e8m1 and `state.vl` was a value that is legal under QEMU's vtype, the AX45MPV vsetvl will reproduce the same vl as long as the hart's VLEN matches QEMU's. This is a pre-existing property of the Tier-B path, not introduced by your change. Not a new bug.

### Item 4 - GPR loop overwrites x5/x6/x7, no leak: CORRECT

GPR loop (lines 107-108) writes x1..x31. x5, x6, x7 all restored from `state.gpr[]`. No scratch leak into the resumed context.

### Item 5 - mcycle/minstret restored at the END after the sweep: CORRECT

Lines 113-119 restore `state.mcycle`/`state.minstret` last, after the sweep. The 32 `vle8.v` + 2 `vsetvl` PB executions increment the hart's hardware counters during halt-mode execution, but those increments are overwritten by the final abstract/PB CSR writes. Resumed counters equal QEMU1's drain values exactly. Correct.

**Caveat (not a bug, a hardware assumption):** this assumes the AX45MPV increments mcycle/minstret during Program Buffer execution at all, OR does not. Either way you overwrite at the end, so it does not matter - which is precisely why doing it last is the right design. Confirmed robust.

## DRAIN side

### Item 6 - all reads first, then PB dump sweep last: CORRECT

`drain_with_passthrough` reads in order: GPRs+pc (via `drain()`), FPRs, then the six V-CSRs (vstart/vxsat/vxrm/vcsr/vtype/vl, lines 71-78), then mcycle/minstret (lines 80-81). Your new dump sweep runs *after* all of these. Because every captured field is already in `state`, the sweep's clobbers (x5/x6/x7, vtype/vl/vstart) and its counter increments cannot corrupt drained state.

Your specific question - "is reading GPRs before clobbering x5/x6/x7 sufficient given QEMU2 restores all GPRs from state.gpr[] anyway?": **CORRECT, and even stronger than you state.** Two independent reasons the clobber is harmless:
1. GPRs x1..x31 are read into `state.gpr[]` by `drain()` *before* the sweep runs, so the captured values are pre-clobber.
2. Even if (1) were not true, QEMU2's `ResumeDriver`/gdbstub restores all 32 GPRs from `state.gpr[]`, so a live-hart clobber of x5/x6/x7 after capture is invisible.

Either reason alone suffices. Ordering is correct.

### Item 6 - **BUG: vstart vs the dump sweep's vse8.v**

This is the one genuine correctness bug across the whole design, on the drain side.

`vse8.v vN,(x7)` is a unit-stride store that begins at element index `vstart` and stores elements `[vstart, vl)`. If the drained hart has `vstart != 0` at the exit trigger, then:

- You read `state.vstart` first (line 72) - good, the value is captured.
- But then your dump sweep does `vsetvl x0,x5,x6` (e8m1, vl=vlenb). **`vsetvl` resets vstart to 0** (RVV spec: every vset{i}vl{i} sets vstart=0). So by the time `vse8.v` runs, vstart is already 0 and the store covers all elements `[0, vlenb)`. The full register is dumped.

So in practice the sweep's own `vsetvl` saves you: vstart is forced to 0 before any `vse8.v`. **The dump is complete.** Confirmed not a bug *as long as the dump sweep issues `vsetvl` before the first `vse8.v`* (which your design does in step "vsetvl e8m1 + 32x vse8.v").

The symmetric concern on RESUME: after your reload sweep, `vle8.v` with vstart=0 (vsetvl reset it) loads all elements. Then the architectural `vstart` restore (line 100) sets `state.vstart` for the resumed program. So a non-zero architectural vstart is preserved into QEMU2's view. Correct.

**Net:** vstart handling is correct *because* both sweeps lead with vsetvl. If you ever drop the per-sweep vsetvl (e.g. "vtype is already e8m1 from a prior op, skip vsetvl"), you reintroduce a partial-store/partial-load bug whenever vstart != 0. Keep the vsetvl in every sweep. Document this dependency.

### Drain side - missing symmetric reorder (gap, not in your 9 items)

You did not mention it, but the icount path is symmetric. `simulator.hpp:492-493` dispatches to either `hybrid_handoff_` or `hybrid_icount_handoff_`, both of which call `drain_with_passthrough` via `HandoffController::on_retire` (handoff_controller.hpp:63). Moving the dump sweep *into* `StateDrain::drain_with_passthrough` (header-only, shared by both controllers) covers both paths in one place. Today the sweep is in the fixture, and `read_v_from_scratch` runs in `simulator.hpp:500-506` (`check_hybrid_handoff`) for both. After your change, `read_v_from_scratch` should still run there (the DM `vse8.v` lands bytes in RAM that `read_v_from_scratch` then copies into `state.vreg[]`). So the data flow becomes: PB `vse8.v` (inside `StateDrain`) -> RAM scratch -> `read_v_from_scratch` (in `simulator.hpp`) -> `state.vreg[]`. Confirm you keep `read_v_from_scratch` in place; only the asm source of the bytes moves from fixture to PB.

## ENCODINGS (verified against RVV 1.0)

All computed and confirmed:

| Encoding | Value | Check |
|---|---|---|
| vtype e8,m1,ta,ma | `0xC0` | vma(b7)=1, vta(b6)=1, vsew[5:3]=000, vlmul[2:0]=000. CORRECT. |
| vle8.v vd,(rs1) formula | `(1<<25)|(rs1<<15)|(vd<<7)|0x07` | CORRECT. |
| vse8.v vs3,(rs1) formula | `(1<<25)|(rs1<<15)|(vs3<<7)|0x27` | CORRECT. |
| **vle8.v v3,(x7)** | **`0x02038187`** | concrete example |
| **vse8.v v3,(x7)** | **`0x020381A7`** | concrete example |
| vle8.v v0,(x7) | `0x02038007` | |
| vse8.v v0,(x7) | `0x02038027` | |
| vle8.v v31,(x7) | `0x02038F87` | |
| vse8.v v31,(x7) | `0x02038FA7` | |
| vsetvl x0,x5,x6 | `0x8062F057` | matches existing `encode_vsetvl(0,5,6)` already in tree |

Cross-check: the fixture today uses I-form `vsetvli zero,a4,e8,m1,ta,ma` which encodes to `0xC077057` with zimm=`0xC0` (a4=x14). That confirms the `0xC0` vtype field is consistent between the I-form your fixture uses and the R-form `vsetvl` your PB design uses. The R-form takes vtype from x6 instead of an immediate, but the bit field is identical (`0xC0`).

**Field detail confirmed for vle8/vse8:** bit25=vm=1 (unmasked), bits[14:12]=width=000 (EEW=8), bit[14:12] mew=0, bits[31:29] nf=000, bits[27:26] mop=00 (unit-stride), bits[24:20] lumop/sumop=00000. The single bit you set at `1<<25` is `vm=1`. The full upper field `mew|nf|...` is all zero, so `1<<25` correctly produces the unmasked unit-stride form. CORRECT. (Note: `vle8.v` and `vse8.v` differ only in the opcode `0x07` vs `0x27`, i.e. LOAD-FP vs STORE-FP. CORRECT.)

## Item 7 - scratch layout / stride: CORRECT

`stage_v_to_scratch` (v_regfile_xfer.hpp:76-82) does one contiguous `memcpy` of `kVScratchBytes = 32 * HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX = 2048` bytes from `state.vreg`. `state.vreg` itself is declared `uint8_t vreg[32 * HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX]` (state_abi.h:76), so register i sits at offset `i * HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX = i*64`.

Therefore the PB pointer stride MUST be `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX` (64), **NOT** vlenb. Your design says stride=64, load-count=vl=vlenb. For VLEN=512, vlenb=64=stride, so they coincide and any choice "happens to work." But the *correct* stride is `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX` because that is the staging stride. CONFIRMED: stride=`HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX`, load `vlenb` bytes per register.

If you ever build with VLEN < 512 (vlenb < 64), the wire format still reserves 64 bytes/reg (the ABI is sized to max). Using stride=vlenb in the PB would then read register i's bytes from the wrong offset (i*vlenb instead of i*64) and desync from the staged layout. **Stride must be the compile-time `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX` constant, never the runtime vlenb.** This matches what the fixture does today (`addi t0, t0, 64` - a hardcoded 64, which equals `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX`).

Loading only vlenb bytes (vl=vlenb via vsetvl e8m1) is correct: vlenb is the true per-register byte count; bytes [vlenb, 64) in each 64-byte slot are padding when VLEN<512 and need not be loaded/stored.

## Item 8 - PB size: CORRECT

Each sweep PB execution is exactly 1 payload instruction (`vle8.v` or `vse8.v`); `exec_progbuf` appends the mandatory `kEbreak`, so 2 slots used out of 16. Far under `kProgbufMaxInsns=15`. No batching needed for correctness.

**Optimization note (not correctness):** you issue 32 separate `exec_progbuf` calls plus 32 abstract x7 writes plus 32 busy-polls per sweep. You *could* batch a few `vle8.v`+`addi` pairs into one PB exec (up to 7 load+addi pairs fit in 15 slots), cutting the number of `exec_progbuf` round-trips ~7x. Not required for correctness; flag for perf if the resume/drain DMI latency matters. The current per-reg-with-abstract-x7-write design is the simplest correct form.

## Item 9 - compiler barrier / ROI hoisting: existing markers SUFFICIENT for correctness; recommend strengthening for benchmarks

Read `rt_c_helpers.h` and `rt_c_v_regs.c`. Analysis:

**For the CORRECTNESS test (asserts `out[i]==expected`):** the test does NOT care which phase computed `y`. The assertions check the closed-form FIR result. Three guarantees keep it correct regardless of barrier removal:
1. `enter_vsim`/`exit_vsim` are `asm volatile(... ::: "memory")` - they block load/store reordering across the marker and force globals to spill/reload.
2. The `asm volatile("" : : "vr"(x))` (line 58) and `"vr"(y)` (line 73) force-live guards pin `x` before `enter_vsim` and `y` before `exit_vsim` into V registers at those exact source points. The compiler cannot dead-code `x`'s phase-1 compute (it is observed by the `vr` asm) and cannot sink `y`'s compute past the `vr(y)` guard.
3. Once the PB sweep round-trips **all 32** V regs bit-identically (just as the fixture's `v_regfile_*_all` did), the compiler's "x lives in v_compiler_choice / y lives in v_compiler_choice" tracking stays valid across the boundary. Whichever physical reg the compiler picked for x and y is preserved by the full-regfile sweep.

So removing `v_regfile_reload_all`/`v_regfile_dump_all` from the fixture does NOT break the correctness test, provided the PB sweep is a faithful full-regfile round-trip. The opaque-call barrier those functions provided is replaced by: (a) the `"memory"` clobber on `enter_vsim`/`exit_vsim`, plus (b) the `"vr"` force-live guards on x and y.

**BUT there is a residual hoisting risk for x and the FIR compute:** the `v_regfile_reload_all()` call sat between `enter_vsim()` and the phase-2 compute (line 61). Being an out-of-line function with a `"memory"` clobber, it was a second barrier *inside* the ROI. Removing it leaves only `enter_vsim()` (also `"memory"`-clobbered) before the FIR. The FIR inputs (`x`) are pinned by `vr(x)` at line 58 *before* `enter_vsim`. The compiler is free to *re-materialize* the FIR compute anywhere after `x` is available, including speculatively before `enter_vsim`, because the FIR (slide/add) instructions are pure (no memory, no volatile) and are not themselves observed until `vr(y)`. The `"memory"` clobber on `enter_vsim` does NOT order pure vector arithmetic - it only orders memory operations. So:

- **Correctness:** still fine. Even if the FIR drifts before `enter_vsim`, the final `y` is correct, and the full-regfile sweep carries whatever V regs are live across the boundary. The `out[]==expected` assertions pass.
- **Benchmark / cycle-accuracy:** **NOT fine.** For a realistic cycle-measuring benchmark, the whole point is that the FIR kernel insns execute *on vsim*, inside the `enter_vsim`/`exit_vsim` window, so their cycles are counted by mcycle. If the compiler hoists the pure vector compute before `enter_vsim` (computed on QEMU1) or sinks it after `exit_vsim` (computed on QEMU2), the kernel's cycles vanish from the ROI. The `"vr"` guards pin the *values* at source points but do not force the *producing instructions* to stay between the markers - the compiler may recompute. The lost out-of-line barrier made hoisting much harder (a real call the optimizer would not speculate across); inline `asm volatile("memory")` markers do not constrain pure RVV arithmetic motion.

**Recommendation:** for benchmark binaries that measure ROI cycles, strengthen `enter_vsim`/`exit_vsim` into **out-of-line, separate-TU, non-inline** functions (declared in a header, defined in their own `.c`, compiled without LTO cross-inlining of the marker body, or marked `__attribute__((noinline,noipa))`). A real call boundary the optimizer cannot see through prevents the pure vector kernel from migrating out of the ROI window. The current inline `asm volatile` + `"vr"` guards are sufficient ONLY for the functional correctness fixture, not for cycle attribution.

For the correctness test specifically (your immediate goal), the existing markers + `"vr"` guards suffice; you can drop the `v_regfile_*_all` calls. For any cycle-measuring use, move the markers out of line.

## Summary table

| Item | Verdict |
|---|---|
| 1 | CORRECT - reorder required; driver needs no RAM access |
| 2 | CORRECT - add x7 to the documented scratch-clobber set; redundant-but-safe double vsetvl |
| 3 | CORRECT |
| 4 | CORRECT |
| 5 | CORRECT |
| 6 | CORRECT - clobber harmless for two independent reasons |
| 6 (vstart) | CORRECT only because each sweep leads with vsetvl (which forces vstart=0). Keep the vsetvl in every sweep or partial load/store reappears when vstart!=0 |
| 7 | CORRECT - stride must be compile-time HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX, never runtime vlenb |
| 8 | CORRECT - 1 insn/exec, no batching needed |
| 9 | Correctness test: markers + "vr" guards SUFFICIENT. Benchmark cycle-accuracy: NOT sufficient, move markers out-of-line |

## Bugs / fixes to apply

1. **Keep `vsetvl` in every PB sweep** (resume reload and drain dump). It is what forces vstart=0 before the vle8.v/vse8.v unit-stride access. Dropping it (e.g. as a perf "optimization" when vtype is already e8m1) reintroduces a partial-element load/store bug whenever the drained hart has vstart != 0. This is the single subtle correctness trap in the design.
2. **Stride = `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX` (64), not vlenb.** Use the compile-time constant for the x7 increment to match the staged layout. Loading vlenb bytes per reg is correct.
3. **Document x7 as scratch.** The existing comments (resume_driver.hpp lines 88-94, 117) promise only x5/x6 are clobbered-then-restored. Your sweep adds x7. The GPR loop restores it, so no leak, but the comment is now stale.
4. **Put the dump sweep inside `StateDrain::drain_with_passthrough`** (header-only, shared by both `hybrid_handoff_` and `hybrid_icount_handoff_`) so both trigger paths get it. Keep `read_v_from_scratch` in `simulator.hpp:500-506` - the PB `vse8.v` writes RAM, then `read_v_from_scratch` copies RAM into `state.vreg[]`. Symmetrically, the reload sweep goes inside `ResumeDriver::resume`, and `stage_v_to_scratch` stays in `simulator.hpp` but moves to *before* `driver.resume()`.

### Critical Files for Implementation
- /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/resume_driver.hpp
- /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/state_drain.hpp
- /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/hybrid/program_buffer.hpp
- /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/verilator/src/simulator.hpp
- /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/tests/fixtures/runtime/v_regfile_xfer.c (the asm being retired; rt_c_v_regs.c will drop the two `v_regfile_*_all` calls)

input.content:
# Phase 5: Transparent V-register transport via Program Buffer

## Context

Today V registers cross the QEMU1 -> vsim -> QEMU2 handoff through a guest-RAM
scratch region (`kVScratchGpa = 0x00FF0000`). The host->RAM staging is done by
vsim (`stage_v_to_scratch` / `read_v_from_scratch`), but the actual
`vle8.v`/`vse8.v` sweep that moves bytes between scratch RAM and the hart's V
regfile is issued by the **fixture binary** in M-mode
(`tests/fixtures/runtime/v_regfile_xfer.c`, called as `v_regfile_reload_all()` /
`v_regfile_dump_all()` in `rt_c_v_regs.c`).

That leaks transport plumbing into every V-using benchmark and forces a fragile
dual literal: the `li t0, 0x00FF0000` in the fixture asm must match the C++
`kVScratchGpa` by hand.

**Goal:** move the `vle8.v`/`vse8.v` sweep into vsim, executed via the RISC-V
Debug Module **Program Buffer (PB)** while the hart is halted. Benchmark
binaries then need zero V-transport code - they just call
`enter_vsim()`/`exit_vsim()`. This is the PB option named in `hybrid_plan.md:67`
and `:141` (the doc's `>100ms`-gated alternative was DPI; we choose PB because it
reuses existing PB infrastructure and needs no per-CPU RTL backdoor like
`set_ilm()`).

**Scope (confirmed): transport only.** ROI-boundary hardening (making
`enter_vsim`/`exit_vsim` out-of-line so a cycle-measuring kernel cannot drift out
of the measured window) is a separate follow-up, NOT part of this phase. The
correctness fixtures stay green with the existing markers + `"vr"` force-live
guards because all 32 V regs round-trip bit-identically.

## Core architecture

The scratch-RAM staging stays. Only the *issuer* of the load/store moves: from
the fixture (M-mode) to vsim (debug mode, via PB). Data source is unchanged.

```
RESUME (inside ResumeDriver::resume, HYBRID_FLAG_V):
  simulator.hpp: stage_v_to_scratch(state.vreg[] -> RAM[0xFF0000])   <-- moved BEFORE resume()
  1. enable mstatus.VS                                  (exists)
  2. NEW reload sweep:
       x5=vlenb, x6=0xC0(e8m1ta ma); vsetvl x0,x5,x6   (vl=vlenb, vstart=0)
       for i in 0..31: x7 = 0xFF0000 + i*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX
                       vle8.v vi,(x7)                    (1-insn PB each)
  3. architectural V-CSR restore (vsetvl from state.vl/vtype, vstart, ...)  (exists; overwrites sweep's vtype/vl/vstart)
  4. GPR loop x1..x31                                    (exists; overwrites x5/x6/x7)
  5. mcycle/minstret restored to exact QEMU1 values      (exists; LAST -> sweep insns cannot pollute counters)

DRAIN (inside StateDrain::drain_with_passthrough, HYBRID_FLAG_V):
  1. read x1..x31+pc, FPR, V-CSRs, mcycle/minstret        (exists; ALL reads first)
  2. NEW dump sweep (LAST):
       x5=vlenb, x6=0xC0; vsetvl x0,x5,x6                 (vstart=0)
       for i in 0..31: x7 = 0xFF0000 + i*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX
                       vse8.v vi,(x7)
  simulator.hpp: read_v_from_scratch(RAM[0xFF0000] -> state.vreg[])  (unchanged, still after drain)
```

Why this ordering is correct (validated):
- **Counters clean:** resume restores mcycle/minstret *after* the sweep; drain
  reads them *before* the sweep. Sweep insns never appear in the measured count.
  No `dcsr.stopcount` dependency.
- **vstart safe:** every sweep leads with `vsetvl`, which forces `vstart=0`, so
  the unit-stride `vle8.v`/`vse8.v` always covers the full register even if the
  drained hart had `vstart != 0`. **Keep the `vsetvl` in every sweep** - dropping
  it reintroduces a partial load/store bug.
- **No GPR leak:** sweep uses x5/x6/x7 as scratch; the GPR loop restores them
  from `state.gpr[]` afterward (resume), or they were already captured before the
  sweep (drain), and QEMU2 restores all GPRs regardless.
- **Stride = compile-time `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX` (64), NOT runtime `vlenb`.**
  Staging lays each reg at `i*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX`; the PB pointer must use the
  same stride. Load count per reg = `vlenb` (vl set via vsetvl e8m1). They
  coincide only at VLEN=512; the constant is the correct one.

## Reuse (do not reinvent)

- `exec_progbuf(bus, span)` - `verilator/src/hybrid/program_buffer.hpp:76`
- `encode_vsetvl(rd,rs1,rs2)` - `program_buffer.hpp:46` (reuse for the e8m1 setup)
- `write_64bit_register(regno,val)` - `resume_driver.hpp:138` (abstract GPR write)
- `regno_gpr(i)` - `abstract_command.hpp:15`
- `kVScratchGpa`, `kVScratchBytes`, `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX` - `v_regfile_xfer.hpp:45,49` / `state_abi.h`
- `stage_v_to_scratch` / `read_v_from_scratch` - `v_regfile_xfer.hpp:76,87` (KEEP - host<->RAM copy unchanged)
- RecordingBus + `regfile` map + progbuf-scan assertion idiom - `tests/hybrid/recording_bus.hpp`, `resume_driver.test.cpp` (Tier-B vsetvl test), `program_buffer.test.cpp`

## Feature breakdown (one per session, TDD RED -> GREEN, structural/behavioral split)

### F1 - RVV unit-stride load/store encoders
- **Behavior:** `encode_vle8_v(vd,rs1)` and `encode_vse8_v(vs3,rs1)` constexpr.
  - `encode_vle8_v = (1u<<25)|((rs1&0x1F)<<15)|((vd&0x1F)<<7)|0x07u`
  - `encode_vse8_v = (1u<<25)|((rs1&0x1F)<<15)|((vs3&0x1F)<<7)|0x27u`
- **RED:** add cases to `tests/hybrid/program_buffer.test.cpp` asserting golden
  hex. Confirmed values: `vle8.v v0,(x7)=0x02038007`, `v3=0x02038187`,
  `v31=0x02038F87`; `vse8.v v0,(x7)=0x02038027`, `v3=0x020381A7`,
  `v31=0x02038FA7`. Cross-check one value with the Andes assembler
  (`echo 'vle8.v v3,(t2)' | riscv64-...-as` + objdump) as ground truth before
  trusting the formula.
- **Files:** `verilator/src/hybrid/program_buffer.hpp` (next to encode_vsetvl),
  `tests/hybrid/program_buffer.test.cpp`.
- **Deps:** none.

### F2 - ResumeDriver PB reload sweep
- **Behavior:** private `reload_v_data(const hybrid_state_v1&)` doing step 2 of
  the resume diagram; call it inside `resume()` under `HYBRID_FLAG_V`, AFTER
  mstatus.VS enable and BEFORE the existing architectural V-CSR block
  (`resume_driver.hpp:90-104`). Use x5/x6 for the e8m1 vsetvl, x7 for the base
  pointer (stride `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX`). Update the line 88-94 / 117 comments
  to list x7 as scratch.
- **RED:** new case in `resume_driver.test.cpp`: set `HYBRID_FLAG_V`,
  `state.vlenb=64`; scan `bus.ops` and assert (a) a `kProgbuf0` write equal to
  `encode_vle8_v(i,7)` exists for every i in 0..31, (b) each is preceded by an
  abstract x7 write of `kVScratchGpa + i*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX`, (c) the whole
  sweep precedes the architectural vsetvl (`encode_vsetvl(0,5,6)` from state.vl)
  and the GPR loop, (d) mcycle/minstret COMMAND writes come after the sweep.
- **Files:** `verilator/src/hybrid/resume_driver.hpp`, `resume_driver.test.cpp`.
- **Deps:** F1.

### F3 - StateDrain PB dump sweep
- **Behavior:** private `dump_v_data(uint64_t vlenb)` doing step 2 of the drain
  diagram; call it at the END of `drain_with_passthrough()` under
  `HYBRID_FLAG_V`, after all reads (`state_drain.hpp:71-81`). Header-only and
  shared, so both the CSR-trigger and icount-trigger controllers get it.
- **RED:** new case in `state_drain.test.cpp`: `input.flags |= HYBRID_FLAG_V`,
  `input.vlenb=64`; assert the `vse8.v` progbuf words appear, and that they come
  AFTER the mcycle/minstret read COMMANDs (so counters/V-CSRs are captured pre-sweep).
- **Files:** `verilator/src/hybrid/state_drain.hpp`, `state_drain.test.cpp`.
- **Deps:** F1.

### F4 - simulator.hpp wiring
- **Behavior:** in `apply_resume_from_qemu` move the `stage_v_to_scratch` call to
  BEFORE `driver.resume()` (`simulator.hpp:204-215`) so the PB reload reads
  populated scratch. Drain side unchanged: `read_v_from_scratch` still runs after
  `on_retire` (`simulator.hpp:500-506`).
- **RED:** no SystemC unit test; this is validated by the F5 e2e gate. Treat the
  reorder as a behavioral change verified end-to-end.
- **Files:** `verilator/src/simulator.hpp`.
- **Deps:** F2, F3.

### F5 - Retire fixture-side transport (the payoff)
- **Behavior:** remove `v_regfile_reload_all()`/`v_regfile_dump_all()` calls and
  the `#include "runtime/v_regfile_xfer.h"` from every fixture that uses them
  (confirmed: `rt_c_v_regs.c`; grep `v_regfile_reload_all|v_regfile_dump_all` for
  `rt_c_v_csrs.c`/`rt_c_v_matmul.c`). Delete
  `tests/fixtures/runtime/v_regfile_xfer.{c,h}` and drop them from
  `tests/fixtures/Makefile` (`RT_C_V_RUNTIME_SRCS`). Keep the `"vr"` force-live
  guards. (Per TDD.md: remove the legacy TU, do not patch it.)
- **RED/gate:** the e2e suite IS the test. Before F2-F4 land, a fixture with the
  calls removed fails the round-trip (V regs do not transport); after F2-F4 it
  passes because vsim does the transport. Run `FILTER=rt_c_v` e2e; it must go
  green with the calls gone.
- **Files:** `tests/fixtures/rt_c_v_regs.c` (+ any other caller),
  `tests/fixtures/runtime/v_regfile_xfer.{c,h}` (delete),
  `tests/fixtures/Makefile`.
- **Deps:** F2, F3, F4.

### F6 - Docs
- **Behavior:** update the "V-State Transport" section of `CLAUDE.md`, the header
  comment in `v_regfile_xfer.hpp` (drop "Phase 5 will replace..."), mark the
  V-transfer item done in `hybrid_plan.md`, and update `docs/USER_GUIDE.md`
  (mandated by `CLAUDE.md`: the fixture-author contract no longer requires
  transport calls). Open a follow-up note for the deferred ROI-boundary hardening.
- **Deps:** F5.

## Verification

Hybrid unit tests are SystemC-free (header-only + ABI), so they build/run via the
existing cmake+ctest flow:

```sh
# unit (per feature)
ctest -R test_program_buffer -V      # F1 encoders
ctest -R test_resume_driver  -V      # F2 reload sweep
ctest -R test_state_drain    -V      # F3 dump sweep

# end-to-end (F4/F5): real 3-phase handoff with PB transport, both trigger modes
JOBS=8 FILTER=rt_c_v bash scripts/run_e2e.sh     # must exit 0
```

End-to-end success criteria:
- `rt_c_v_regs` PASS in both `csr` and `pc` modes with the fixture calling only
  `enter_vsim()`/`exit_vsim()` (no transport asm).
- `rt_c_v_regs.elf` no longer links `v_regfile_xfer.c` (it is deleted).
- The QEMU2 `out[]` verification still matches the closed-form FIR, proving all
  32 V regs round-tripped via the PB sweep.

## Out of scope (follow-up)

ROI-boundary hardening: convert `enter_vsim`/`exit_vsim` into out-of-line,
separate-TU functions so a cycle-measuring kernel cannot be hoisted/sunk out of
the measured window. Needed only for benchmark cycle-attribution fidelity, not

# Phase 5: Transparent V-register transport via Program Buffer

## Context

Today V registers cross the QEMU1 -> vsim -> QEMU2 handoff through a guest-RAM
scratch region (`kVScratchGpa = 0x00FF0000`). The host->RAM staging is done by
vsim (`stage_v_to_scratch` / `read_v_from_scratch`), but the actual
`vle8.v`/`vse8.v` sweep that moves bytes between scratch RAM and the hart's V
regfile is issued by the **fixture binary** in M-mode
(`tests/fixtures/runtime/v_regfile_xfer.c`, called as `v_regfile_reload_all()` /
`v_regfile_dump_all()` in `rt_c_v_regs.c`).

That leaks transport plumbing into every V-using benchmark and forces a fragile
dual literal: the `li t0, 0x00FF0000` in the fixture asm must match the C++
`kVScratchGpa` by hand.

**Goal:** move the `vle8.v`/`vse8.v` sweep into vsim, executed via the RISC-V
Debug Module **Program Buffer (PB)** while the hart is halted. Benchmark
binaries then need zero V-transport code - they just call
`enter_vsim()`/`exit_vsim()`. This is the PB option named in `hybrid_plan.md:67`
and `:141` (the doc's `>100ms`-gated alternative was DPI; we choose PB because it
reuses existing PB infrastructure and needs no per-CPU RTL backdoor like
`set_ilm()`).

**Scope (confirmed): transport only.** ROI-boundary hardening (making
`enter_vsim`/`exit_vsim` out-of-line so a cycle-measuring kernel cannot drift out
of the measured window) is a separate follow-up, NOT part of this phase. The
correctness fixtures stay green with the existing markers + `"vr"` force-live
guards because all 32 V regs round-trip bit-identically.

## Core architecture

  4. GPR loop x1..x31                                    (exists; overwrites x5/x6/x7)
  5. mcycle/minstret restored to exact QEMU1 values      (exists; LAST -> sweep insns cannot pollute counters)

DRAIN (inside StateDrain::drain_with_passthrough, HYBRID_FLAG_V):
  1. read x1..x31+pc, FPR, V-CSRs, mcycle/minstret        (exists; ALL reads first)
  2. NEW dump sweep (LAST):
       x5=vlenb, x6=0xC0; vsetvl x0,x5,x6                 (vstart=0)
       for i in 0..31: x7 = 0xFF0000 + i*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX

  3. architectural V-CSR restore (vsetvl from state.vl/vtype, vstart, ...)  (exists; overwrites sweep's vtype/vl/vstart)
  4. GPR loop x1..x31                                    (exists; overwrites x5/x6/x7)
  5. mcycle/minstret restored to exact QEMU1 values      (exists; LAST -> sweep insns cannot pollute counters)

DRAIN (inside StateDrain::drain_with_passthrough, HYBRID_FLAG_V):
  1. read x1..x31+pc, FPR, V-CSRs, mcycle/minstret        (exists; ALL reads first)
  2. NEW dump sweep (LAST):
       x5=vlenb, x6=0xC0; vsetvl x0,x5,x6                 (vstart=0)
       for i in 0..31: x7 = 0xFF0000 + i*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX
                       vse8.v vi,(x7)
  simulator.hpp: read_v_from_scratch(RAM[0xFF0000] -> state.vreg[])  (unchanged, still after drain)
```

Why this ordering is correct (validated):
- **Counters clean:** resume restores mcycle/minstret *after* the sweep; drain
  reads them *before* the sweep. Sweep insns never appear in the measured count.
  No `dcsr.stopcount` dependency.
- **vstart safe:** every sweep leads with `vsetvl`, which forces `vstart=0`, so
  the unit-stride `vle8.v`/`vse8.v` always covers the full register even if the
  drained hart had `vstart != 0`. **Keep the `vsetvl` in every sweep** - dropping
  it reintroduces a partial load/store bug.
- **No GPR leak:** sweep uses x5/x6/x7 as scratch; the GPR loop restores them
  from `state.gpr[]` afterward (resume), or they were already captured before the
  sweep (drain), and QEMU2 restores all GPRs regardless.
- **Stride = compile-time `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX` (64), NOT runtime `vlenb`.**
  Staging lays each reg at `i*HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX`; the PB pointer must use the
  same stride. Load count per reg = `vlenb` (vl set via vsetvl e8m1). They
  coincide only at VLEN=512; the constant is the correct one.

- `encode_vsetvl(rd,rs1,rs2)` - `program_buffer.hpp:46` (reuse for the e8m1 setup)
- `write_64bit_register(regno,val)` - `resume_driver.hpp:138` (abstract GPR write)
- `regno_gpr(i)` - `abstract_command.hpp:15`
- `kVScratchGpa`, `kVScratchBytes`, `HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX` - `v_regfile_xfer.hpp:45,49` / `state_abi.h`
- `stage_v_to_scratch` / `read_v_from_scratch` - `v_regfile_xfer.hpp:76,87` (KEEP - host<->RAM copy unchanged)

  - `encode_vse8_v = (1u<<25)|((rs1&0x1F)<<15)|((vs3&0x1F)<<7)|0x27u`

  hex. Confirmed values: `vle8.v v0,(x7)=0x02038007`, `v3=0x02038187`,
  `v31=0x02038F87`; `vse8.v v0,(x7)=0x02038027`, `v3=0x020381A7`,

  (`echo 'vle8.v v3,(t2)' | riscv64-...-as` + objdump) as ground truth before

  `tests/hybrid/program_buffer.test.cpp`.

### F2 - ResumeDriver PB reload sweep
- **Behavior:** private `reload_v_data(const hybrid_state_v1&)` doing step 2 of

  diagram; call it at the END of `drain_with_passthrough()` under

```

  `enter_vsim()`/`exit_vsim()` (no transport asm).

  32 V regs round-tripped via the PB sweep.
